infobox.value: (Brazilian Portuguese: [[ˈklubi dʒi ʁeˈɡataz du flaˈmẽɡu]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese"); lit. 'Flamengo Rowing Club'), more commonly referred to as simply **Flamengo**, is a Brazilian multi-sports club based in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), in the neighborhood of [Gávea](/wiki/G%C3%A1vea "Gávea"). It was founded and named after the [Flamengo](/wiki/Flamengo,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Flamengo, Rio de Janeiro") neighborhood in 1895 and is best known for its professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") team. Flamengo is one of two clubs to have [never been relegated](/wiki/List_of_unrelegated_association_football_clubs "List of unrelegated association football clubs") from the [top division](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), along with [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_FC "São Paulo FC"), and the most popular football club in Brazil with more than 46.9 million fans, equivalent to 21.9% of the population that supports a team in Brazil.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Clube de Regatas do Flamengo** (Brazilian Portuguese: [[ˈklubi dʒi ʁeˈɡataz du flaˈmẽɡu]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese"); lit. 'Flamengo Rowing Club'), more commonly referred to as simply **Flamengo**, is a Brazilian multi-sports club based in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), in the neighborhood of [Gávea](/wiki/G%C3%A1vea "Gávea"). It was founded and named after the [Flamengo](/wiki/Flamengo,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Flamengo, Rio de Janeiro") neighborhood in 1895 and is best known for its professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") team. Flamengo is one of two clubs to have [never been relegated](/wiki/List_of_unrelegated_association_football_clubs "List of unrelegated association football clubs") from the [top division](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), along with [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_FC "São Paulo FC"), and the most popular football club in Brazil with more than 46.9 million fans, equivalent to 21.9% of the population that supports a team in Brazil.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The club was first established in 1895 specifically as a [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") club in the Flamengo neighborhood and did not play its first official football match until 1912. Flamengo's traditional uniform features red and black striped shirts with white shorts, and red and black striped socks. Flamengo has typically played its home matches in the [Maracanã Stadium](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium") (which also often host the [Brazil national team](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team")) since its completion in 1950. Since 1969, the [vulture](/wiki/New_World_vulture "New World vulture") (Portuguese: *urubu*) has been the [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot") of Flamengo.

Flamengo is Brazil's richest and most valuable football club with an annual revenue of [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")1.2 billion (€218 million)[[3]](#cite_note-3) and a valuation of over R$5,096 billion (€800 million).[[4]](#cite_note-4) Flamengo is the non-European football club with the most followers on social media, with 65 million followers across all platforms as of 2 December 2025,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and also the most successful Brazilian team of the 21st century so far.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The club is also one of the most successful in Brazilian football, having amassed a considerable array of national and international titles.

The clubs's training center, officially known as "[Ninho do Urubu](/wiki/Ninho_do_Urubu "Ninho do Urubu")" ("Vulture's Nest"), is located in [Vargem Grande](/wiki/Vargem_Grande,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Vargem Grande, Rio de Janeiro"), Rio de Janeiro. It serves as the primary training facility for the Flamengo football club, housing its professional teams and [youth academy](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_(youth) "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo (youth)").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Flamengo's youth academy is one of the most prolific in Brazil and in the world, having developed a number of Brazilian internationals such as [Zico](/wiki/Zico_(footballer) "Zico (footballer)"), [Zizinho](/wiki/Zizinho "Zizinho"), [Vinícius Júnior](/wiki/Vin%C3%ADcius_J%C3%BAnior "Vinícius Júnior"), [Lucas Paquetá](/wiki/Lucas_Paquet%C3%A1 "Lucas Paquetá"), [Júlio César](/wiki/J%C3%BAlio_C%C3%A9sar_(football_goalkeeper,_born_1979) "Júlio César (football goalkeeper, born 1979)"), [Adriano](/wiki/Adriano_(footballer,_born_February_1982) "Adriano (footballer, born February 1982)"), [Mário Zagallo](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Zagallo "Mário Zagallo"), [Júnior](/wiki/L%C3%A9o_J%C3%BAnior "Léo Júnior") and [Leonardo](/wiki/Leonardo_Ara%C3%BAjo "Leonardo Araújo").

Flamengo has also been well represented in the Brazil national team; at the [1938 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup"), forward [Leônidas da Silva](/wiki/Le%C3%B4nidas_(footballer,_born_1913) "Leônidas (footballer, born 1913)"), a Flamengo player at the time, was the [Golden Boot](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_Golden_Boot "FIFA World Cup Golden Boot") winner with 7 goals and won the [Golden Ball](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_Golden_Ball "FIFA World Cup Golden Ball"), thus becoming the first Brazilian player ever to win those two awards. Twelve years later at the [1950 World Cup](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup"), Zizinho, a [midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder "Midfielder") for Flamengo, also won the Golden Ball after he was voted best player; 4 out of the 10 top scorers for Brazil have all been Flamengo players at one point in their careers, seven players have won the World Cup whilst playing for Flamengo, and Flamengo player Mário Zagallo scored Brazil's third goal in the [1958 World Cup final](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup_final "1958 FIFA World Cup final").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of CR Flamengo](/wiki/History_of_CR_Flamengo "History of CR Flamengo")

### Establishment of the club (1895–1912)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Establishment of the club (1895–1912)")]

Flamengo was founded on 17 November 1895, by a group of rowers gathered at club member Nestor de Barros's manor on [Flamengo Beach](/wiki/Flamengo,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Flamengo, Rio de Janeiro") in Rio de Janeiro. In the late 19th century, [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") was the elite, upper middle class sport in the region and the group hoped to impress the young women of the city's high society by establishing a rowing club. Previously, they could only afford a used boat named *Pherusa*, which had to be completely rebuilt before it could be used in competition. The team debuted on 6 October 1895, when they sailed off the [Caju Point](/wiki/Caju,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Caju, Rio de Janeiro") toward Flamengo Beach. However, strong winds turned over the boat and the rowers nearly drowned. They were rescued by a fishing boat named *Leal* (Loyal). Later as the *Pherusa* was undergoing repairs, it was stolen and never found again. The group saved money to buy a new boat, the *Etoile*, renamed *Scyra*.

On the night of 17 November, the group gathered at Nestor de Barros's manor on Flamengo beach and founded the *Grupo de Regatas do Flamengo* (English: *Flamengo Rowing Group*) and elected its first board and president (Domingos Marques de Azevedo). The name was changed a few weeks later to its current title of *Clube de Regatas do Flamengo* (*Flamengo Rowing Club*). The founders decided that the anniversary of the club's foundation should be commemorated on 15 November to coincide with [Republic Proclamation Day](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Republic_(Brazil) "Proclamation of the Republic (Brazil)"), a national holiday.

Flamengo's football team was only established after a group of ten dissatisfied players from [Fluminense](/wiki/Fluminense_FC "Fluminense FC") broke away from that club following a board dispute.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The players decided to join Flamengo because Alberto Borgerth, the team's captain, was also a rower for Flamengo. Also, establishing a land sports department at Flamengo was preferable to joining football rivals [Botafogo](/wiki/Botafogo_de_Futebol_e_Regatas "Botafogo de Futebol e Regatas") or the all-English club [Paissandu](/wiki/Paissandu_Atl%C3%A9tico_Clube "Paissandu Atlético Clube"). The new members were admitted on 8 November 1911. A motion against the club taking part in football tournaments was put to a vote but was defeated, and as a result the members officially established the club's new football department on 24 December 1911.

### Football in the amateur era (1912–1933)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Football in the amateur era (1912–1933)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Flamengo_1912.jpg/250px-Flamengo_1912.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flamengo_1912.jpg)

The recently formed [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") team before a match vs. Paissandu, 1912

The new team trained on [Russel Beach](/w/index.php?title=Russel_Beach&action=edit&redlink=1 "Russel Beach (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Praia_do_Russel "pt:Praia do Russel")] and gradually gained the support of the locals, who closely watched their practice matches. The first official match was played on 3 May 1912, and marked, to this day, the largest margin of victory in the club's history, as they defeated [Mangueira](/wiki/Sport_Club_Mangueira "Sport Club Mangueira") 16–2. Flamengo's first ever match against [Fluminense](/wiki/Fluminense_FC "Fluminense FC"), the start of the [Fla-Flu](/wiki/Fla-Flu "Fla-Flu") rivalry, was played on 7 July of that year and was won by Fluminense by a score of 3–2. That same year, Flamengo finished as runners-up of the [Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca"), the Rio de Janeiro State Championship. The team's first uniform was nicknamed the "*papagaio vintém*", due to its similarity to a particular type of kite.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/1914_-_Flamengo.jpg/250px-1914_-_Flamengo.jpg)](/wiki/File:1914_-_Flamengo.jpg)

The Flamengo team of 1914, when the club won its first [Carioca championship](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca")

In 1914 the club won the Campeonato Carioca for the first time, dressed in a red, black, and white-striped shirt nicknamed the "*cobra coral*" ([coral snake](/wiki/Coral_snake "Coral snake")) was worn until 1916. Flamengo won the Campeonato Carioca again the following year, in 1915, and secured their first back-to-back championship; something they also did with their titles in 1920 and 1921.

In 1925, the team won the Campeonato Carioca and five other tournaments, a record at the time. In 1927 the prominent Rio newspaper [Jornal do Brasil](/wiki/Jornal_do_Brasil "Jornal do Brasil"), in partnership with a mineral water company, held a mail-in contest to find "the most beloved club in Brazil." Though Flamengo enjoyed their largest increase in fan support after the club professionalized in the 1930s, they still defeated popular rivals [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/CR_Vasco_da_Gama "CR Vasco da Gama") in the vote.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11) This was the first of many times that Flamengo would be polled as the nation's most popular club, originating the nickname "*O mais querido do Brasil*" ("the most beloved of Brazil").[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12) In 1933 the team went on its first tour outside Brazil (to [Montevideo](/wiki/Montevideo "Montevideo") and [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires")[[13]](#cite_note-13)) and on 14 May of the same year played its final match as an amateur team, defeating [River Futebol Clube](/w/index.php?title=River_Futebol_Clube&action=edit&redlink=1 "River Futebol Clube (page does not exist)") by a score of 16–2.[[14]](#cite_note-14) After this, the club's football department became professional.

### Early professional era (1934–1955)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early professional era (1934–1955)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Flamengo_football_team_1934.jpg/250px-Flamengo_football_team_1934.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flamengo_football_team_1934.jpg)

Flamengo's team, 1934. [National Archives of Brazil](/wiki/Brazilian_National_Archives "Brazilian National Archives")

Local advertiser [José Bastos Padilha](/w/index.php?title=Jos%C3%A9_Bastos_Padilha&action=edit&redlink=1 "José Bastos Padilha (page does not exist)") was elected club president in 1934 and served until 1937. Under his tenure, the club massively improved its popularity in both Rio de Janeiro and the entirety of Brazil. For publicity, he organized a contest for students in schools to create phrases describing Flamengo, from which the phrase *uma vez Flamengo, Flamengo até morrer* ("Once you are Flamengo, you are Flamengo 'til you die") was developed and would later be adopted as part of the club's anthem. In 1936 Padilha signed excellent players such as [Domingos da Guia](/wiki/Domingos_da_Guia "Domingos da Guia") and [Leônidas da Silva](/wiki/Le%C3%B4nidas_(footballer,_born_1913) "Leônidas (footballer, born 1913)") (who would go on to be the leading goalscorer in the [1938 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup") as a Flamengo player). These beloved players endeared Flamengo to the public and it is believed that by this time Flamengo was the most popular club in the country.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11) In 1937 Flamengo hired Hungarian coach [Izidor "Dori" Kürschner](/wiki/Izidor_K%C3%BCrschner "Izidor Kürschner"), who introduced the [WM system](/wiki/Formation_(association_football)#WM "Formation (association football)") to Brazil and other innovations from Europe such as training without the use of the ball and playing a more defensive, controlled style. Padilha facilitated the construction of Flamengo's new stadium and current training center, the [Estádio da Gávea](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_da_G%C3%A1vea "Estádio da Gávea"). The stadium was inaugurated on 4 September 1938, when [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/CR_Vasco_da_Gama "CR Vasco da Gama") defeated Flamengo 2–0 and Kürschner was promptly fired.

In 1938, the five-year split in Rio de Janeiro football over the dispute between professionalism and amateurism was resolved with the merger of the two competing leagues (Flamengo had been a member of the professional LCF – *Liga Carioca de Football*). In 1939, after twelve years without winning any titles, Flamengo conquered the state championship with a team that would become the basis of the three-time state champions in the 1940s.

In 1941, the group played its first international competition, the Hexagonal Tournament of Argentina.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 1942, the first organized supporters group in all of Brazil, Charanga Rubro-Negra, was founded in support of Flamengo.[[16]](#cite_note-organizadasbrasil.com-16) Flamengo's popularity grew incidentally during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") when Brazil's allies, the United States, installed two high-powered antennas in [Natal](/wiki/Natal,_Rio_Grande_do_Norte "Natal, Rio Grande do Norte") and [Belém](/wiki/Bel%C3%A9m "Belém") in the north of Brazil to intercept enemy radio signals.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12) They also allowed residents in the North and Northeast regions to receive the radio broadcasts of football matches. As Rio de Janeiro was the national capital at the time and Flamengo was highly successful in the war years with [Zizinho](/wiki/Zizinho "Zizinho") and Domingos da Guia, nationwide support increased. In 1944, Flamengo completed their first *tricampeonato Carioca*: three consecutive Rio de Janeiro state titles (winning the 1942, 1943, and 1944 competitions).[[17]](#cite_note-:2-17) The key player of this squad was Zizinho, a player developed at Flamengo and considered the first ever "idol" of the club. Zizinho was transferred to [Bangu](/wiki/Bangu_Atl%C3%A9tico_Clube "Bangu Atlético Clube") just before the start of the [1950 World Cup](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup") in Brazil, where he scored twice and the *Seleção* finished runners-up. From 1953 to 1955, Flamengo once again won the [Rio de Janeiro State League](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca") three consecutive times.[[17]](#cite_note-:2-17)

### Zico and the world champions (1974–1983)

[1981 Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/1981_Intercontinental_Cup "1981 Intercontinental Cup") starting lineup

Flamengo won their 18th [Campeonato Carioca state championship](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca") in 1978. The following five years would come to represent the club's most glorious era. Brazilian stars like [Júnior](/wiki/Leovegildo_Lins_da_Gama_J%C3%BAnior "Leovegildo Lins da Gama Júnior"), [Carpegiani](/wiki/Paulo_C%C3%A9sar_Carpegiani "Paulo César Carpegiani"), [Adílio](/wiki/Ad%C3%ADlio_(footballer,_born_1956) "Adílio (footballer, born 1956)"), [Cláudio Adão](/wiki/Cl%C3%A1udio_Ad%C3%A3o "Cláudio Adão") and [Tita](/wiki/Milton_Queiroz_da_Paix%C3%A3o "Milton Queiroz da Paixão") were led by [Zico](/wiki/Zico_(footballer) "Zico (footballer)") to become state champions three times in a row – the club's third tri-championship. This run of sustained excellent play pushed Flamengo towards its first [Brazilian Championship in 1980](/wiki/1980_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1980 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"). As national champions, the club qualified to play in the South American continental tournament, the [1981 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/1981_Copa_Libertadores "1981 Copa Libertadores"), for the first time.

The [1981 season](/wiki/1981_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "1981 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season") is a benchmark year in Flamengo's history.[[18]](#cite_note-18) They advanced through the semi-final group stage of the Copa Libertadores with four victories in four matches.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In the final they encountered Chilean club [Cobreloa](/wiki/Cobreloa "Cobreloa"), also a debutante club in the tournament. In the first final at the [Maracanã](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium"), Flamengo prevailed (2–1) with two goals from Zico. At the [National Stadium](/wiki/Estadio_Nacional_Julio_Mart%C3%ADnez_Pr%C3%A1danos "Estadio Nacional Julio Martínez Prádanos") in Santiago the following week, the Brazilian team received a violent reception on the field and fell 1–0 from a [free kick](/wiki/Direct_free_kick "Direct free kick").[[20]](#cite_note-:12-20)[[21]](#cite_note-:23-21) Equal on goals, a third match was played at the neutral venue of the [Estadio Centenario](/wiki/Estadio_Centenario "Estadio Centenario") in Montevideo.[[21]](#cite_note-:23-21) Zico scored twice in the first half, sealing the game and the championship. Flamengo were crowned champions of South America on 23 November and qualified for the [Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/Intercontinental_Cup_(1960%E2%80%932004) "Intercontinental Cup (1960–2004)"), a single match to be played in Tokyo's [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Stadium,_Tokyo "Olympic Stadium, Tokyo") against [European Champions' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") winner [Liverpool F.C.](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.").

On 13 December 1981, Zico, [Tita](/wiki/Tita_(footballer,_born_1958) "Tita (footballer, born 1958)"), and [Nunes](/wiki/Nunes_(footballer) "Nunes (footballer)") took the field for the most important match in the club's history. Two goals by Nunes and one by Adílio (all in the first half) along with a brilliant [midfield](/wiki/Midfielder "Midfielder") performance by Zico earned Flamengo the title of first Brazilian World Champions since [Pelé](/wiki/Pel%C3%A9 "Pelé")'s [Santos](/wiki/Santos_Futebol_Clube "Santos Futebol Clube"), shutting out Liverpool 3–0.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The following two years were also marked with success. One more Rio de Janeiro State Championship in 1981 and two back-to-back Brazilian Championships – [1982](/wiki/1982_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1982 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") and [1983](/wiki/1983_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1983 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") – closed Flamengo's "Golden Age."[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### National success and the return of Zico (1984–1994)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: National success and the return of Zico (1984–1994)")]

Main article: [1987 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A](/wiki/1987_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1987 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Zico_flamengo_elgrafico.jpg/250px-Zico_flamengo_elgrafico.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zico_flamengo_elgrafico.jpg)

[Zico](/wiki/Zico_(footballer) "Zico (footballer)") played for Flamengo from 1971 to 1983 and 1985–89, setting several records for the club.

After spending two years in Italy playing for [Udinese](/wiki/Udinese_Calcio "Udinese Calcio"), Zico returned to Flamengo in 1986 and won his last state championship. Only one month after returning, he suffered a severe knee injury after a violent tackle from [Bangu](/wiki/Bangu_Atl%C3%A9tico_Clube "Bangu Atlético Clube") defender Marcio Nunes, which interrupted his career for several months and affected his form in the [1986 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup").

In 1987, Zico was a major contributor to Flamengo's victory in the first edition of the [Copa União](/wiki/Copa_Uni%C3%A3o "Copa União"). That year, the [CBF](/wiki/Brazilian_Football_Confederation "Brazilian Football Confederation") was experiencing serious financial and institutional crises and was unable to secure sponsorship to organize the national championship as in years prior. As a result, the thirteen biggest clubs in Brazil (which included Flamengo) reacted and created a new entity named the [Club of 13](/wiki/Clube_dos_13 "Clube dos 13") to organize a championship of their own. The CBF originally supported the decision by the Club of 13, but were pressured by other clubs to create a larger national tournament. As a result, CBF placed three additional clubs into the Copa União, regarded the Copa União as the "Green Module," and organized a second "Yellow Module" of 16 other teams. CBF then decided that for the [1987 Brazilian Championship](/wiki/1987_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1987 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), the winners and runners-up of both modules would face each other in a knockout-style cup to determine the national champion and qualification for the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/Copa_Libertadores "Copa Libertadores"), although this decision was made after the beginning of the championship, without Club of 13 agreement. With strong performances from [Zico](/wiki/Zico_(footballer) "Zico (footballer)"), [Zé Carlos](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Carlos_da_Costa_Ara%C3%BAjo "José Carlos da Costa Araújo"), [Renato Gaúcho](/wiki/Renato_Portaluppi "Renato Portaluppi") and [Bebeto](/wiki/Bebeto "Bebeto"), Flamengo conquered the Copa União with major victories over [Internacional](/wiki/Sport_Club_Internacional "Sport Club Internacional") and [Atlético Mineiro](/wiki/Clube_Atl%C3%A9tico_Mineiro "Clube Atlético Mineiro"). However, there was a dispute over whether Flamengo and Internacional of the Green Module would dispute the quadrangular against [Sport Recife](/wiki/Sport_Club_do_Recife "Sport Club do Recife") and [Guarani](/wiki/Guarani_FC "Guarani FC") of the Yellow Module. The Club of 13 clubs had agreed to not participate in the final set up by the CBF, since it was decided while the matches were already being played, but [Eurico Miranda](/wiki/Eurico_Miranda "Eurico Miranda"), a representative of [Vasco](/wiki/CR_Vasco_da_Gama "CR Vasco da Gama"), Flamengo's archi-rival and member of the Club of 13, had already signed an agreement with CBF regarding the final, without the board consent. Flamengo still did not participate in the final under the understanding that it would only determine the entrants of the Copa Libertadores and not the Brazilian national champion.[[24]](#cite_note-24) CBF officially recognized Sport as the sole champion in 1987 and they qualified to the Copa Libertadores. In 2011, CBF retroactively declared Flamengo champion of 1987.[[25]](#cite_note-25) However, Sport later appealed the decision to a Common Justice Tribunal, which is prohibited by FIFA, and CBF ultimately declared Sport as the sole champion of that year, pending appeals from Flamengo, all of which were unsuccessful, and a few years later Sport was officially declared as the champion of the 1987 season.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Throughout his career at Flamengo, Zico scored 508 goals and was the top scorer in club history before retiring in 1990.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Even without its biggest star, the early years of the post-Zico era were successful for Flamengo. They achieved national victory in the second edition of the [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/Copa_do_Brasil "Copa do Brasil") in [1990](/wiki/1990_Copa_do_Brasil "1990 Copa do Brasil"), defeating [Goiás](/wiki/Goi%C3%A1s_Esporte_Clube "Goiás Esporte Clube") in the finals. In 1992, Flamengo won their [fifth Campeonato Brasileiro](/wiki/1992_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1992 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), defeating [Botafogo](/wiki/Botafogo_de_Futebol_e_Regatas "Botafogo de Futebol e Regatas") across two legs in the final (3–0, 2–2). The team's key player was again [Júnior](/wiki/Leovegildo_Lins_da_Gama_J%C3%BAnior "Leovegildo Lins da Gama Júnior") at 38 years old.

### Mediocre campaigns and relegation escapes (1995–2005)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mediocre campaigns and relegation escapes (1995–2005)")]

After winning the Brazilian League title in 1992, the club entered a major financial crisis and domestic and international achievements became less frequent, although the team still won trophies ranked less in importance. In [1993](/wiki/1993_Supercopa_Libertadores "1993 Supercopa Libertadores"), Flamengo lost the [Supercopa Libertadores finals](/wiki/1993_Supercopa_Libertadores_finals "1993 Supercopa Libertadores finals") to Sao Paulo. In 1995, the year of Flamengo's centenary, radio sports broadcaster [Kléber Leite](/wiki/Kl%C3%A9ber_Leite "Kléber Leite") became chairman of the club and signed striker [Romário](/wiki/Rom%C3%A1rio "Romário"), the current [FIFA World Player of the Year](/wiki/FIFA_World_Player_of_the_Year "FIFA World Player of the Year"), from [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona").[[29]](#cite_note-29) He joined [Sávio](/wiki/S%C3%A1vio "Sávio") and later [Edmundo](/wiki/Edmundo "Edmundo") to become, as the supporters called, "the attack of dreams".[[30]](#cite_note-30) Even with Romário and other stars, Flamengo's centennial year did not yield major trophies, and the club struggled in the league, finishing 21st of 24. Flamengo only won the [Taça Guanabara](/wiki/Ta%C3%A7a_Guanabara "Taça Guanabara"), the first phase of the [state league](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca"),[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) and finished runners-up of [1995 Supercopa Libertadores](/wiki/1995_Supercopa_Libertadores "1995 Supercopa Libertadores"). However, in 1996, Flamengo went undefeated in the Campeonato Carioca. Sávio was the top scorer and best player in Flamengo's victorious [1996 Copa de Oro](/wiki/1996_Copa_de_Oro "1996 Copa de Oro") campaign. The Copa de Oro was Flamengo's first international success since 1981, their third overall international title.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 1997 Flamengo finished trophyless for one of the few times in its history, but overall had a good season, finishing fifth in the [Serie A](/wiki/1997_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1997 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), finishing runner-up to [Grêmio](/wiki/Gr%C3%AAmio_FBPA "Grêmio FBPA") in the Copa do Brasil on away goals, and finishing runner-up in the [Torneo Rio-Sao Paulo](/wiki/1997_Torneio_Rio-S%C3%A3o_Paulo "1997 Torneio Rio-São Paulo"). 1998 was another trophyless season, but far worse than 1997; *Mengão* finished 11th in the [Serie A](/wiki/1998_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1998 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), were knocked out of the [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/1998_Copa_do_Brasil "1998 Copa do Brasil") with a 5–0 loss to [Vitoria](/wiki/EC_Vitoria "EC Vitoria"), and failed to advance out of the group stages of the [Copa Mercosur](/wiki/1998_Copa_Mercosur "1998 Copa Mercosur"), although top scorer Romario continued to show individual brilliance with 35 goals from 40 appearances.

In 1999, [Edmundo dos Santos Silva](/wiki/Edmundo_dos_Santos_Silva "Edmundo dos Santos Silva") was elected club president, and brought with him a massive contract with sports marketing company [ISL](/wiki/International_Sport_and_Leisure "International Sport and Leisure").[[34]](#cite_note-34) Despite a mediocre campaign in that year's [Campeonato Brasileiro](/wiki/1999_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1999 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), finishing 12th, the club lifted the [1999 Copa Mercosur](/wiki/1999_Copa_Mercosur "1999 Copa Mercosur") at [Estádio Palestra Itália](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Palestra_It%C3%A1lia "Estádio Palestra Itália") after beating Palmeiras 7–6 on aggregate in an exciting two legged final, and were successful at the regional level, winning the Campeonato Carioca over rivals Vasco, who had won the 1998 Copa Libertadores.

2000 was also a mediocre year for the club, being knocked out of the [Copa Mercosur](/wiki/2000_Copa_Mercosur "2000 Copa Mercosur") by River Plate in the quarter-finals, and exiting the [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/2000_Copa_do_Brasil "2000 Copa do Brasil") with a heavy 8–2 aggregate loss to Santos. However the club were successful at the regional level again, beating Vasco 5–1 on aggregate for a back-to-back championship.

In [2001](/wiki/2001_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "2001 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") league play, Flamengo avoided relegation to the [Série B](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_B "Campeonato Brasileiro Série B") by beating [Palmeiras](/wiki/Sociedade_Esportiva_Palmeiras "Sociedade Esportiva Palmeiras") 2–0 on the final matchday.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Despite near relegation, the club had a decent season overall; they won the [2001 Copa dos Campeões](/wiki/2001_Copa_dos_Campe%C3%B5es "2001 Copa dos Campeões") inter-state title, finished runner up in the [2001 Copa Mercosur](/wiki/2001_Copa_Mercosur "2001 Copa Mercosur") to [San Lorenzo](/wiki/San_Lorenzo_de_Almagro "San Lorenzo de Almagro") on penalties, and beat Vasco again in the state league with a late free-kick goal by [Dejan Petković](/wiki/Dejan_Petkovi%C4%87 "Dejan Petković"). With the state title, the club became *tricampeão* (three-time champion) for the fourth time in its history (1999-2000-2001) .

In 2002, ISL went bankrupt, for reasons unrelated to their contract with Flamengo, and the club was left without its wealthy partner. In the same year, Edmundo Santos Silva was removed from his role as president in a controversial manner amidst accusation of impropriety.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Lacking the funds to make key signings, Flamengo failed to field competitive teams and narrowly avoided relegation in the 2002, 2004, and 2005 campaigns, with the 2005 season being one of the worst in Flamengo's history; the club only escaped relegation after the arrival of coach [Joel Santana](/wiki/Joel_Santana "Joel Santana"), who directed the team to six wins and three draws in nine matches played under his command. Twice in this low period, the team reached back-to-back Copa do Brasil finals, in [2003](/wiki/2003_Copa_do_Brasil "2003 Copa do Brasil") and [2004](/wiki/2004_Copa_do_Brasil "2004 Copa do Brasil"), ultimately falling to [Cruzeiro](/wiki/Cruzeiro_Esporte_Clube "Cruzeiro Esporte Clube") and [Santo André](/wiki/Esporte_Clube_Santo_Andr%C3%A9 "Esporte Clube Santo André") respectively. This Flamengo has also evaded massive taxes in the [Banestado scandal](/wiki/Banestado_scandal "Banestado scandal") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Esc%C3%A2ndalo_do_Banestado "pt:Escândalo do Banestado")].[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### End of title drought (2006–2018)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: End of title drought (2006–2018)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Adriano_Leite_Ribeiro.jpg/250px-Adriano_Leite_Ribeiro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adriano_Leite_Ribeiro.jpg)

[Adriano](/wiki/Adriano_Leite_Ribeiro "Adriano Leite Ribeiro") celebrating a goal for Flamengo. In 2009 he finished as joint top-scorer in Série A with 19 goals.

In 2006, Flamengo reached the [Copa do Brasil final](/wiki/2006_Copa_do_Brasil "2006 Copa do Brasil") for a fifth time, finally managing to conquer the title after losing three previous finals, this time beating rivals [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/CR_Vasco_da_Gama "CR Vasco da Gama"). From 2007 to 2009 Flamengo completed their fifth *tricampeonato* in the [Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca"), and became sole owners of the record for most Carioca titles with 31 ([Fluminense](/wiki/Fluminense_FC "Fluminense FC") had 30 at the time).

On 9 March 2007, Flamengo received a commemorative date on the [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") official calendar. Governor [Sérgio Cabral Filho](/wiki/S%C3%A9rgio_Cabral_Filho "Sérgio Cabral Filho") declared 17 November (the day the club was founded) "Flamengo Day".

In the 2007 Copa Libertadores, after a near-perfect group stage where the club was undefeated, expectations were high for the team. However, the club was eliminated by Uruguayan minnows [Defensor Sporting](/wiki/Defensor_Sporting "Defensor Sporting") in the first knockout round.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In the [Campeonato Brasileiro](/wiki/2007_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "2007 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), Flamengo spent most of the season in mid table hoping for at least a top ten finish but in the last five matchdays climbed to second place before being defeated by [Náutico](/wiki/Clube_N%C3%A1utico_Capibaribe "Clube Náutico Capibaribe") 1–0 in the final round and ultimately ending the season third, which was good enough for a spot in the following year's Copa Libertadores.

In the [2008 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2008_Copa_Libertadores "2008 Copa Libertadores"), Flamengo began their campaign by topping their group. After defeating [Club América](/wiki/Club_Am%C3%A9rica "Club América") 4–2 at [Estadio Azteca](/wiki/Estadio_Azteca "Estadio Azteca"), in what was Flamengo's first tournament tie against a Mexican club, Flamengo looked to be one of the strongest sides in the competition. However, Flamengo polemically lost the second leg at home 3–0 and were eliminated in the round of 16 again, in what was Joel Santana's final match with the club.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) In the [Brasileirao](/wiki/2008_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "2008 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), Flamengo finished fifth, and in [2009](/wiki/2009_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "2009 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") despite being in tenth place in midseason, Flamengo won the league title for the first time since 1992. With this victory Flamengo became five-time league champions, seventeen seasons after their last title.[[42]](#cite_note-netfla2-42) The 2009 championship team finished the season with 67 points, the lowest winning point total in Brazil since the current league format was established in 2003. Flamengo were champions despite spending only two rounds at the top of the league: the final two, and the title was won after a dramatic 2–1 comeback victory against [Grêmio](/wiki/Gr%C3%AAmio_Foot-Ball_Porto_Alegrense "Grêmio Foot-Ball Porto Alegrense") in the final round.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Ronaldinho_Ga%C3%BAcho.jpg/250px-Ronaldinho_Ga%C3%BAcho.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ronaldinho_Ga%C3%BAcho.jpg)

[Ronaldinho](/wiki/Ronaldinho "Ronaldinho") celebrates scoring for Flamengo in February 2011.

Flamengo experienced a poor run in Série A from 2010 to 2015, finishing better than tenth only once. Following the success of 2009, the club gambled on winning several titles and signed striker [Vágner Love](/wiki/V%C3%A1gner_Love "Vágner Love") to form a pair with [Adriano](/wiki/Adriano_Leite_Ribeiro "Adriano Leite Ribeiro"). The dream of repeating as state champions four times in a row was foiled by Botafogo in 2010. After narrowly qualifying out of the group stage in the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2010_Copa_Libertadores "2010 Copa Libertadores"), manager [Andrade](/wiki/Andrade_(footballer,_born_1957) "Andrade (footballer, born 1957)") was still fired. In their first quarter-final appearance since 1993, after disposing of Corinthians, Flamengo were eliminated by [Universidad de Chile](/wiki/Club_Universidad_de_Chile "Club Universidad de Chile") on away goals. Shortly after, Vágner Love and Adriano left the team. A series of coaching changes during the troublesome domestic league saw Flamengo survive relegation, avoiding defeat in their final nine matches, and claim the final berth to the [Copa Sudamericana](/wiki/2011_Copa_Sudamericana "2011 Copa Sudamericana") under manager [Vanderlei Luxemburgo](/wiki/Vanderlei_Luxemburgo "Vanderlei Luxemburgo").

The blockbuster signing of 2011 was 30-year-old superstar [Ronaldinho](/wiki/Ronaldinho "Ronaldinho") from [A.C. Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan"). He was joined by Argentine [Darío Bottinelli](/wiki/Dar%C3%ADo_Bottinelli "Darío Bottinelli") and Fluminense idol [Thiago Neves](/wiki/Thiago_Neves "Thiago Neves"). Flamengo won the Campeonato Carioca outright in an undefeated campaign, but captured no other trophies that season: eliminations in the [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/2011_Copa_do_Brasil "2011 Copa do Brasil") by [Ceará](/wiki/Cear%C3%A1_Sporting_Club "Ceará Sporting Club"), a heavy 5–0 loss in the [Sudamericana](/wiki/2011_Copa_Sudamericana "2011 Copa Sudamericana") by Universidad de Chile, and a fourth-place finish in the [league](/wiki/2011_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "2011 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") left fans feeling that a strong roster had been squandered. The season saw the retirement of Serbian club idol [Dejan Petković](/wiki/Dejan_Petkovi%C4%87 "Dejan Petković") as well. In 2012 Ronaldinho sued Flamengo claiming lack of payment for four months and canceled his contract with the club,[[44]](#cite_note-44) Thiago Neves returned to Fluminense after a drawn-out negotiation with contract-holders [Al-Hilal](/wiki/Al-Hilal_FC "Al-Hilal FC"), and defender [Alex Silva](/wiki/Alex_Sandro_da_Silva "Alex Sandro da Silva") was loaned to [Cruzeiro](/wiki/Cruzeiro_Esporte_Clube "Cruzeiro Esporte Clube") after threatening Flamengo with a lawsuit. Vágner Love and [Ibson](/wiki/Ibson "Ibson") returned for a 2012 campaign that yielded no trophies and a group-stage exit from the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2012_Copa_Libertadores "2012 Copa Libertadores").

At the end of 2012, Flamengo elected Eduardo Bandeira de Mello as club president for three years. The goal of his term was to improve the club's finances, after an independent audit assessed Flamengo's debt at [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")750 million.[[45]](#cite_note-45) After a typical series of managerial changes, [Jayme de Almeida](/wiki/Jayme_de_Almeida "Jayme de Almeida") was appointed as interim manager during which he fought off relegation and won the [2013 Copa do Brasil final](/wiki/2013_Copa_do_Brasil "2013 Copa do Brasil") against [Atlético Paranaense](/wiki/Club_Athletico_Paranaense "Club Athletico Paranaense"). It was Flamengo's third Copa title, after 1990 and 2006.

Flamengo's [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/Copa_do_Brasil "Copa do Brasil") title-defense fell short to [Atlético Mineiro](/wiki/Clube_Atl%C3%A9tico_Mineiro "Clube Atlético Mineiro") in the semi-final. However, by 2014, Flamengo was the only club that successfully reduced their debt over the year (down to [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")600 million) and recorded the highest annual profit.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In 2015 after an inconsistent start to the Carioca and national league seasons, multiple managers were dismissed and Flamengo failed to qualify for the Libertadores. However, Flamengo had signed [Paolo Guerrero](/wiki/Paolo_Guerrero "Paolo Guerrero") and [Ederson](/wiki/Ederson_(footballer,_born_January_1986) "Ederson (footballer, born January 1986)") and were the most valuable club in Brazil with debt now reduced to [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")495 million.[[47]](#cite_note-47) As a result, president Bandeira was re-elected. The club signed fan-favorite [Diego](/wiki/Diego_(footballer,_born_1985) "Diego (footballer, born 1985)") in the mid-season and mounted a strong campaign, but could not catch [Palmeiras](/wiki/Sociedade_Esportiva_Palmeiras "Sociedade Esportiva Palmeiras") in 2016.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

2017 was characterized as the year Flamengo played two major finals at the end of the season but failed to win either. After going undefeated in the [2017 Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/2017_Campeonato_Carioca "2017 Campeonato Carioca"), they were eliminated in the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2017_Copa_Libertadores "2017 Copa Libertadores") group stage, failing to win a single match away from home but qualifying for the [Copa Sudamericana](/wiki/2017_Copa_Sudamericana "2017 Copa Sudamericana") in third place. In the [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/2017_Copa_do_Brasil "2017 Copa do Brasil"), the club reached the final where they lost in a penalty shootout to Cruzeiro. Less than three months later, they reached an unprecedented [Copa Sudamericana final](/wiki/2017_Copa_Sudamericana_Finals "2017 Copa Sudamericana Finals"). They lost away to [Independiente](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_Independiente "Club Atlético Independiente") and drew at home 1–1, losing the title. After the match, a group of Flamengo supporters rioted outside the hotel where Independiente were staying. [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL") punished the club with two closed-door home matches in the following [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/Copa_Libertadores "Copa Libertadores").[[49]](#cite_note-49) In the league, the club finished sixth and qualified for the following year's Libertadores.

Nine years after their last Campeonato Brasileiro victory, Flamengo made a title run but fell just short. In [2018](/wiki/2018_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "2018 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") they spent the most rounds as league leader (thirteen) and broke their points record from 2016 (72), but finished runners-up behind Palmeiras. In the Libertadores they finished runner-up behind eventual champion River Plate and qualified to the round of 16, where they were eliminated by Cruzeiro. That season, the club recorded their two highest outgoing transfer fees in history: 18-year-old winger [Vinícius Júnior](/wiki/Vin%C3%ADcius_J%C3%BAnior "Vinícius Júnior")[[50]](#cite_note-50) moved to [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_CF "Real Madrid CF") in July for €46 million, and 20-year-old [midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder "Midfielder") [Lucas Paquetá](/wiki/Lucas_Paquet%C3%A1 "Lucas Paquetá")[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) transferred to A.C. Milan for a reported €35 million at the end of the year. Both were products of [Flamengo's youth academy](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_Youth_Academy "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo Youth Academy").

Main article: [2019 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season](/wiki/2019_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "2019 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season")

On the morning of 8 February 2019 a fire erupted at Flamengo's [Ninho do Urubu training center](/wiki/Ninho_do_Urubu "Ninho do Urubu"), popular name for Centro de Treinamento George Helal (Vulture's Nest training center) ([Flamengo training ground fire](/wiki/Flamengo_training_ground_fire "Flamengo training ground fire")).[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) The fire resulted in the deaths of ten academy players between the ages of 14 and 17 training with the club. Three others were injured. The cause of the fire was a malfunctioning [air-conditioning unit](/wiki/Air_conditioning "Air conditioning") that caught fire in the room of one of the victims close to 5:00 am. President Rodolfo Landim described it as "the worst tragedy the club has ever experienced in its 123 years."[[56]](#cite_note-56) The [governor](/wiki/Wilson_Witzel "Wilson Witzel") of the state of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") declared a three-day period of mourning following the tragedy.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Since then, Flamengo fans sing in memory of those kids, usually referred to as the "Garotos do Ninho". It happens every tenth minute of Flamengo home games, since 10 kids died in the tragedy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/20191205_cpupo_flaxava_303-scaled.jpg/330px-20191205_cpupo_flaxava_303-scaled.jpg)](/wiki/File:20191205_cpupo_flaxava_303-scaled.jpg)

[Adriano](/wiki/Adriano_(footballer,_born_February_1982) "Adriano (footballer, born February 1982)"), [Juan](/wiki/Juan_(footballer,_born_1979) "Juan (footballer, born 1979)") and [Adílio](/wiki/Ad%C3%ADlio_(footballer,_born_1956) "Adílio (footballer, born 1956)"), great players of the club, holding the 2019 [Campeonato Brasileiro Série A](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"), [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/Copa_Libertadores "Copa Libertadores") and [Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca") trophies.

The [2019 season](/wiki/2019_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "2019 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season") marked the most successful one in the club's footballing history. At the end of 2018, [Rodolfo Landim](/wiki/Rodolfo_Landim "Rodolfo Landim") was elected club president for a three-year term. Flamengo paid the most expensive incoming transfer fee for a player in Brazilian football history, signing [Giorgian de Arrascaeta](/wiki/Giorgian_de_Arrascaeta "Giorgian de Arrascaeta")[[58]](#cite_note-58) from [Cruzeiro](/wiki/Cruzeiro_Esporte_Clube "Cruzeiro Esporte Clube") for [R$](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real")63 million (€14.5 million). In January the club signed forward [Bruno Henrique](/wiki/Bruno_Henrique_Pinto "Bruno Henrique Pinto") from [Santos](/wiki/Santos_FC "Santos FC") and secured the loan of striker [Gabriel Barbosa](/wiki/Gabriel_Barbosa "Gabriel Barbosa") from [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan").[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

After advancing out of the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2019_Copa_Libertadores "2019 Copa Libertadores") group stage, manager [Abel Braga](/wiki/Abel_Braga "Abel Braga") resigned and Flamengo hired Portuguese manager [Jorge Jesus](/wiki/Jorge_Jesus "Jorge Jesus").[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo-62) Europe-based players [Rafinha](/wiki/Rafinha_(footballer,_born_1985) "Rafinha (footballer, born 1985)"), [Filipe Luís](/wiki/Filipe_Lu%C3%ADs "Filipe Luís"), [Pablo Marí](/wiki/Pablo_Mar%C3%AD "Pablo Marí") and [Gerson](/wiki/Gerson_(footballer,_born_1997) "Gerson (footballer, born 1997)") were added to play alongside Flamengo's other record signings.[[63]](#cite_note-:9-63)[[64]](#cite_note-:14-64) After qualifying to their first Copa Libertadores semi-final since 1984, Flamengo defeated [Grêmio](/wiki/Gr%C3%AAmio_Foot-Ball_Porto_Alegrense "Grêmio Foot-Ball Porto Alegrense") 5–0 in their home leg at the Maracanã to advance their first Copa Libertadores final since 1981. For the first time in Copa Libertadores history, the final was played as a single match in a neutral venue. On 23 November 2019, at the [Estadio Monumental](/wiki/Estadio_Monumental_%22U%22 "Estadio Monumental \"U\"") in [Lima, Peru](/wiki/Lima "Lima") against defending champions [River Plate](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_River_Plate "Club Atlético River Plate"), Flamengo trailed 0–1 in the final minutes before Gabriel scored twice to secure the [2–1 victory](/wiki/2019_Copa_Libertadores_Final "2019 Copa Libertadores Final").[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Less than 24 hours later, Flamengo became champions of the [Campeonato Brasileiro Série A](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") for the first time since 2009 with four matches in hand after a loss by [Palmeiras](/wiki/Sociedade_Esportiva_Palmeiras "Sociedade Esportiva Palmeiras") to Grêmio in the 34th round. Flamengo became only the second Brazilian club to win their state championship ([2019 Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/2019_Campeonato_Carioca "2019 Campeonato Carioca")), Campeonato Brasileiro, and Copa Libertadores in the same season, after [Pelé](/wiki/Pel%C3%A9 "Pelé")'s 1962 Santos team. Flamengo's 2019 campaign under Jorge Jesus' leadership broke a number of records in the Campeonato Brasileiro's 20-team double round-robin era (2006–present): most points (90), most wins (28), most goals scored (86), best goal differential (+49), longest undefeated streak (24 matches), most points clear of runners-up (16) and most goals by a single player (25 from Gabriel Barbosa).[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Flamengo participated in the [FIFA Club World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Club_World_Cup "FIFA Club World Cup") for the first time in the club's history in [2019](/wiki/2019_FIFA_Club_World_Cup "2019 FIFA Club World Cup") in Qatar. The club defeated [Al Hilal SFC](/wiki/Al_Hilal_SFC "Al Hilal SFC") 3–1 in the semi-final, but lost 0–1 to [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") in the [final](/wiki/2019_FIFA_Club_World_Cup_Final "2019 FIFA Club World Cup Final").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/David_luis_libertadores.jpg/330px-David_luis_libertadores.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_luis_libertadores.jpg)

[David Luiz](/wiki/David_Luiz "David Luiz") holding the [2022 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2022_Copa_Libertadores "2022 Copa Libertadores") trophy.

#### 2020 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020 season")]

Main article: [2020 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season](/wiki/2020_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "2020 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season")

After winning the revived [Supercopa do Brasil](/wiki/2020_Supercopa_do_Brasil "2020 Supercopa do Brasil") against [Athletico Paranaense](/wiki/Club_Athletico_Paranaense "Club Athletico Paranaense"), then the [Recopa Sudamericana](/wiki/2020_Recopa_Sudamericana "2020 Recopa Sudamericana") against [Copa Sudamericana](/wiki/2019_Copa_Sudamericana "2019 Copa Sudamericana") champions [Independiente del Valle](/wiki/C.S.D._Independiente_del_Valle "C.S.D. Independiente del Valle"), and the [2020 Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/2020_Campeonato_Carioca "2020 Campeonato Carioca"), in July 2020 Jorge Jesus departed from Flamengo to return to [Benfica](/wiki/S.L._Benfica "S.L. Benfica"), having won five titles in Brazil. Jesus's successor was former [Pep Guardiola](/wiki/Pep_Guardiola "Pep Guardiola") assistant [Domènec Torrent](/wiki/Dom%C3%A8nec_Torrent "Domènec Torrent"), but his tenure was brief and he was replaced with [Rogério Ceni](/wiki/Rog%C3%A9rio_Ceni "Rogério Ceni") in November 2020. Ceni led Flamengo to a second consecutive Campeonato Brasileiro championship, finishing one point ahead of [Internacional](/wiki/Sport_Club_Internacional "Sport Club Internacional").

#### 2021 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2021 season")]

Main article: [2021 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season](/wiki/2021_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "2021 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season")

In [2021](/wiki/2021_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "2021 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season") Ceni led Flamengo to a third consecutive Campeonato Carioca, but was released after four losses in Flamengo's first ten Campeonato Brasileiro Série A matches. The club signed [Renato Gaúcho](/wiki/Renato_Ga%C3%BAcho "Renato Gaúcho") as manager, who brought strong results in league play and took Flamengo back to the [Copa Libertadores Final](/wiki/2021_Copa_Libertadores_Final "2021 Copa Libertadores Final") against Palmeiras, but lost 2–1 in extra time on 27 November 2021.[[68]](#cite_note-68) He and the club parted ways after.

Main article: [2022 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season](/wiki/2022_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "2022 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season")

In [2022](/wiki/2022_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "2022 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season"), after a difficult start to the year under manager [Paulo Sousa](/wiki/Paulo_Sousa "Paulo Sousa") (runners-up in the Campeonato Carioca and Supercopa do Brasil), [Dorival Júnior](/wiki/Dorival_J%C3%BAnior "Dorival Júnior") returned to Flamengo and brought another wave of glory. The team advanced to the finals of the [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/2022_Copa_do_Brasil_Finals "2022 Copa do Brasil Finals") against [Corinthians](/wiki/Sport_Club_Corinthians_Paulista "Sport Club Corinthians Paulista"). In the second leg in the Maracanã, tied 1–1 on aggregate, Flamengo were victorious in the penalty shootout with [Rodinei](/wiki/Rodinei "Rodinei") scoring the winner. This was Flamengo's fourth [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/Copa_do_Brasil "Copa do Brasil") championship and first since 2013. Later that month on 29 October 2022, Flamengo faced [Athletico Paranaense](/wiki/Club_Athletico_Paranaense "Club Athletico Paranaense") in [Guayaquil, Ecuador](/wiki/Guayaquil "Guayaquil") for the [final of the Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2022_Copa_Libertadores_Final "2022 Copa Libertadores Final"). Gabriel Barbosa scored the only goal of the match and Flamengo claimed their second Copa Libertadores in four years, and third overall.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Crest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Crest")]

Flamengo's crest has changed slightly throughout the club's history. Most of the changes has been changes to the interlocked letters monogram, with the latest redesign being unveiled in 2018.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The club uses three crests in different situations: the full crest is used as the club's official logo, the rowing crest is used for all rowing related uniforms and equipment, and the white "CRF" monogram is typically the only component of the crest worn on the primary football uniform. It remarkably resembles the "RFC" monogram traditionally used by Scottish club [Rangers F.C.](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.").

Beginning in 1980, Flamengo wore three white stars aligned vertically along the side of their monogram crest to indicate their three state league tri-championships (1942–43–44, 1953–54–55, and 1978–79–79 Special).[[71]](#cite_note-71) When [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") became Flamengo's kit provider in 2000, their first kit featured the full shield crest with three stars above it for the first time. After the fourth state league tri-championship (1999-2000-2001) and to commemorate the 20th anniversary of the [1981 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/1981_Copa_Libertadores "1981 Copa Libertadores") and [Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/1981_Intercontinental_Cup "1981 Intercontinental Cup") championships, a fourth white star and a gold star were introduced above the crest. Since 2005 the club uses only the gold star above the "CRF" monogram crest on their shirts.

### Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/40px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg)

Wikimedia Commons has media related to [Clube de Regatas do Flamengo kits](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_kits "commons:Clube de Regatas do Flamengo kits").

At the 1895 meeting which established the Flamengo rowing club, the club's official colors were decided as blue and gold to symbolized the sky of Rio de Janeiro and the riches of Brazil.[[72]](#cite_note-:02-72) The team adopted a uniform of thick blue and gold horizontal stripes. However Flamengo failed to win a single regatta in their first year and gained the nickname of "bronze club." The team colors were perceived as bad luck, and the colored fabric was expensive to import from England. One year after the club's establishment, the official colors were replaced with the current red and black.

In 1912, at the request of the Flamengo rowing team (who opposed the use of their same uniform by the newly established football team), the football players dressed in shirts divided into red and black quarters which became known as the *papagaio de vintém* uniform, named after a particular style of [kite](/wiki/Kite "Kite"). However the shirt became synonymous with bad luck and was replaced in 1913 by a shirt with red and black horizontal stripes and thinner white bands. This uniform was nicknamed the *cobra coral* due to its similarity to the pattern of a [coral snake](/wiki/Coral_snake "Coral snake"). This was the uniform worn when Flamengo won their first [Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca") title in 1914. The white bands were removed from the shirt in 1916 as the pattern was very similar to the [flag of Germany at the time](/wiki/List_of_German_flags#German_Empire_(1871–1918) "List of German flags"), who Brazil was allied against in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). The rowing team permitted the football team to use their same uniform, and Flamengo's traditional football uniform of a red and black striped shirt, white shorts and red-black socks was born.[[73]](#cite_note-:3-73)

In 1938, Flamengo manager [Dori Kürschner](/wiki/Izidor_K%C3%BCrschner "Izidor Kürschner") suggested the creation of a secondary white uniform to "improve the visibility in night matches." The new uniform was approved by the club, and Flamengo became a pioneer of secondary uniforms in Brazil. The white shirt had two red and black stripes across the chest until 1979 when it was changed to a plain white chest with stripes on the sleeves. This was the shirt worn by the team that won the [1981 Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/1981_Intercontinental_Cup "1981 Intercontinental Cup").[[73]](#cite_note-:3-73)

Beginning in the 1990s the club began to experiment with their second and third alternative uniforms, sometimes wearing all black or all red shirts.[[74]](#cite_note-74) In 1995 for the club's centenary, a "*papagaio de vintém*" shirt was worn in friendlies.[[73]](#cite_note-:3-73) In 2010 uniform supplier [Olympikus](/wiki/Olympikus "Olympikus") introduced a blue and gold alternative uniform which paid homage to Flamengo's original colors and regatta uniform, however it was not well received by fans who likened it to the uniform worn by the fictional satirical team "Tabajara" on the popular comedy program [Casseta & Planeta Urgente](/wiki/Casseta_%26_Planeta "Casseta & Planeta").[[72]](#cite_note-:02-72)[[75]](#cite_note-75) In the first half of the [2009 season](/wiki/2009_Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_season "2009 Clube de Regatas do Flamengo season"), the team wore a uniform without sponsorship for the first time in 25 years.[[73]](#cite_note-:3-73) Flamengo have continued to traditionally wear red and black striped shirts with white shorts as their primary uniform.
