### Scarlet-Black Nation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Scarlet-Black Nation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Ta%C3%A7a_Guanabara_de_2008_%28Final_-_Flamengo_2-1_Botafogo.jpg/250px-Ta%C3%A7a_Guanabara_de_2008_%28Final_-_Flamengo_2-1_Botafogo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ta%C3%A7a_Guanabara_de_2008_(Final_-_Flamengo_2-1_Botafogo.jpg)

Flamengo supporters at Maracanã stadium.

Since the early 1990s, surveys have shown that Flamengo is consistently the most supported club in Brazil with an estimated more than 40 million fans. In a 2019 survey, 20 percent of adult football fans in Brazil consider themselves supporters of Flamengo, with high levels of support in all states of the country, including the [North](/wiki/North_Region,_Brazil "North Region, Brazil") and [Northeast regions](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil"), in addition to Rio de Janeiro. Flamengo supporters are known as *Nação Rubro-Negra* (en: *Scarlet-Black Nation*).

The first organized supporters group in all of Brazil, Charanga Rubro-Negra (*Scarlet-Black Charanga Band*), was founded in support of Flamengo in 1942.[[16]](#cite_note-organizadasbrasil.com-16) Since then, a large number of additional organized supporters groups have formed around Flamengo, notably Torcida Jovem-Fla (*Young-Fla*), Urubuzada (*Vultures*), Flamanguaça (*FlaBooze*), and Raça Rubro-Negra (*Scarlet-Black Race*).

In 2007 Flamengo supporters were declared as part of the cultural heritage of the city of Rio de Janeiro, along with [bossa nova](/wiki/Bossa_nova "Bossa nova") and Bola Preta, the oldest [Carnival block](/wiki/Carnival_block "Carnival block") in Rio.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

In the [1983 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A](/wiki/1983_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1983 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") final, Flamengo played against [Santos](/wiki/Santos_FC "Santos FC") in the [Maracanã](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium") in front of an official crowd of 155,523 with some estimates of over 160,000 people in attendance.

The largest attendance for a football match in the world's history was the derby between Flamengo and Fluminense in 1963, with 194,603 spectators. Flamengo matches in the Maracanã have broken the 150,000 attendance mark thirteen times.

In July 2020, their YouTube channel FLATV passed [Liverpool F.C.](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") as the club with the third-most subscriptions for a football channel only behind [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") and [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Flamengo's channel reached over 5 million subscriptions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Mascot")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Mosaico_flamengo.jpg/330px-Mosaico_flamengo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mosaico_flamengo.jpg)

Mosaic of Flamengo fans showing the vulture mascot with the most important cups won by the club

Flamengo's first [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot") was [Popeye the Sailor Man](/wiki/Popeye "Popeye"), a comic book and cartoon character from the 1940s. The idea for the mascot came from Argentine cartoonist Lorenzo Molas, who saw in Popeye the strength and persistence of Flamengo, in addition to its obvious connection with the sea. However, such a mascot was never very popular among the club's supporters.

In the 1960s, rival fans began to call Flamengo fans *urubus* (English: "vultures"), a racist allusion to the large mass of Afro-descendant and poor supporters. Such an offensive nickname was never well received by Flamengo fans, until 31 May 1969.[[121]](#cite_note-urubu-121) It was on a Sunday, when a Scarlet-Black fan decided to take a [vulture](/wiki/New_World_vulture "New World vulture") to a game between Flamengo and [Botafogo](/wiki/Botafogo_FR "Botafogo FR") at [Maracanã](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium"). At the time, the two clubs were playing the classic with the greatest post-[Garrincha](/wiki/Garrincha "Garrincha") rivalry. And Flamengo hadn't beaten the rival for four years. In the stands, Botafogo fans shouted, as always, that Flamengo was a *urubu* team.[[121]](#cite_note-urubu-121)

The vulture was released in the stands with a Flamengo's flag tied to its feet and, when it landed on the field, just before the game started, the crowd cheered and shouted: *É urubu, é urubu.* (English: "it's a vulture, it's a vulture"). Flamengo won the game 2–1 and, from there, the new mascot was consecrated, taking Popeye's place. The cartoonist [Henfil](/wiki/Henfil "Henfil"), tried to humanize him in his sports cartoons in newspapers and magazines, and the *Urubu* became a popular mascot.[[121]](#cite_note-urubu-121)

In 2000, Flamengo's mascot received an official design and a name: *Samuca*.[[122]](#cite_note-122) However, this name did not become popular among the supporters, who continue to call him simply *Urubu*.

On 25 May 2008, debuted at Maracanã in a match between Flamengo and [Internacional](/wiki/SC_Internacional "SC Internacional"), valid for the [2008 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A](/wiki/2008_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "2008 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A"). Since then, they have been present in several Flamengo games and events.[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)

### Anthem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Anthem")]

Flamengo has two anthems: the official one, called *Hymno Rubro-Negro* (English: "Scarlet-Black Anthem"), which was created in 1920 with lyrics and music by Paulo Magalhães (former goalkeeper of the club), recorded in 1932 by singer [Castro Barbosa](/wiki/Castro_Barbosa "Castro Barbosa") and registered in 1937 at the Instituto Nacional de Música, with the refrain "*Flamengo! Flamengo! Tua glória é lutar, Flamengo! Flamengo! Campeão de terra e mar*" (in English: "Flamengo! Flamengo! Your glory is to fight, Flamengo! Flamengo! Champion of land and sea"); and the popular one, with lyrics and music by [Lamartine Babo](/wiki/Lamartine_Babo "Lamartine Babo"), recorded for the first time by [Gilberto Alves](/wiki/Gil_(footballer,_born_1950) "Gil (footballer, born 1950)") in 1945. The latter is the best known and the one that sings the glories of the club, whose refrain is "*Uma vez Flamengo, sempre Flamengo*" (English: "Once you are Flamengo, always Flamengo").[[125]](#cite_note-125)

## Stadiums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Stadiums")]

### Rua Paysandu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Rua Paysandu")]

Flamengo's first official home ground was the **Estádio da Rua Paysandu** ('Paysandu Street Stadium'). The ground formerly belonged to [Paissandu Atlético Clube](/wiki/Paissandu_Atl%C3%A9tico_Clube "Paissandu Atlético Clube") before they ceased playing football in 1914. The owners of the ground, the [Guinle family](/wiki/Eduardo_Palassin_Guinle "Eduardo Palassin Guinle"), rented the field to Flamengo where they played their home matches from 1915 to 1932.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Between 1912 and 1915 (and later between 1932 and 1938), the club played all their matches on the grounds of [Botafogo](/wiki/Botafogo_de_Futebol_e_Regatas "Botafogo de Futebol e Regatas") or [Fluminense](/wiki/Fluminense_FC "Fluminense FC"). The first Flamengo match at Rua Paysandu was played on 31 October 1915, in the Campeonato Carioca against [Bangu](/wiki/Bangu_Atl%C3%A9tico_Clube "Bangu Atlético Clube"). Crowds of 15,000 watched Flamengo face Fluminese at the park in 1918 and 1919.

### Estádio da Gávea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Estádio da Gávea")]

:   Main article: [Estádio da Gávea](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_da_G%C3%A1vea "Estádio da Gávea")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/G%C3%A1vea_HPIM0892.JPG/250px-G%C3%A1vea_HPIM0892.JPG)](/wiki/File:G%C3%A1vea_HPIM0892.JPG)

Estádio da Gávea

Flamengo's home stadium is nominally the **Estádio da Gávea** (officially named the **Estádio José Bastos Padilha** at Flamengo's Gávea Headquarters), which was inaugurated on 4 September 1938, and has a capacity of 4,000 people. The stadium is named after José Bastos Padilha, Flamengo's president at the time of the stadium's construction, from 1933 to 1937. Even though Flamengo no longer play their matches at Gávea, the site serves as the club's administrative headquarters. Since the 1990s, the stadium has been used almost exclusively for the club's youth and women's teams' matches, and as the training ground for the senior team. Most matches are played at the significantly larger [Maracanã Stadium](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_do_Maracan%C3%A3 "Estádio do Maracanã"), considered by supporters as the real Flamengo home ground.[[127]](#cite_note-127) Gávea Stadium is not actually located in the neighborhood of [Gávea](/wiki/G%C3%A1vea "Gávea") but rather in [Leblon](/wiki/Leblon "Leblon").

During the [2014 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup"), the [Dutch National Team](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") used the [Estádio da Gávea](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_da_G%C3%A1vea "Estádio da Gávea") and all of its facilities as their training ground in preparation for the competition.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

### Maracanã

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Maracanã")]

Main article: [Maracanã Stadium](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Maracan%C3%A3_stadium.jpg/250px-Maracan%C3%A3_stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maracan%C3%A3_stadium.jpg)

Inside view of Maracanã

Since its construction for the [1950 World Cup](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup"), the **Maracanã** has primarily served as the home ground for the four biggest Rio de Janeiro clubs. The stadium was officially completed in 1965, 17 years after construction began. In 1963, more than 194,000 people attended a match between Flamengo and Fluminense at the Maracanã. The capacity of the stadium allowed Flamengo to have the largest support of any clubs in Brazil for much of the 20th century.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) In 1989 [Zico](/wiki/Zico_(footballer) "Zico (footballer)") scored his final goal in the historic stadium, setting the current unbroken record for goals in the Maracanã at 333. An upper stand in the stadium collapsed on 19 July 1992, in the second match of the finals of [1992 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A](/wiki/1992_Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "1992 Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") between [Botafogo](/wiki/Botafogo_de_Futebol_e_Regatas "Botafogo de Futebol e Regatas") and Flamengo, leading to the death of three spectators and injuring 50 others.[[131]](#cite_note-131) Following the disaster, the stadium's capacity was greatly reduced as it was converted to an all-seater stadium in the late 1990s. Following its 50th anniversary in 2000, the stadium underwent renovations which would increase its full capacity to around 103,000. After years of planning and nine months of closure between 2005 and 2006 (during which Flamengo played their home matches at [Volta Redonda](/wiki/Volta_Redonda_Futebol_Clube "Volta Redonda Futebol Clube")'s [Estádio Raulino de Oliveira](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Raulino_de_Oliveira "Estádio Raulino de Oliveira") and [Portuguesa](/wiki/Associa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Atl%C3%A9tica_Portuguesa_(RJ) "Associação Atlética Portuguesa (RJ)")'s [Estádio Luso Brasileiro](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Luso_Brasileiro "Estádio Luso Brasileiro")), the stadium was reopened in January 2007 with an all-seated capacity of 87,000. For the 2014 World Cup and 2016 Olympics and Paralympics, a major reconstruction project was initiated in 2010. The original seating bowl, with a two-tier configuration, was demolished, giving way to a new one-tier seating bowl.[[132]](#cite_note-stadiumguide.com-132)

The stadium is officially under the management of Brazilian conglomerate [Odebrecht](/wiki/Odebrecht "Odebrecht") as of 2013.[[133]](#cite_note-133) This has resulted in unfavorable rental agreements for Flamengo who do not officially administer the stadium and often owe rental fees for matches in excess of their ticket revenue, even for matches with high attendance.[[134]](#cite_note-134) The most recent rental agreement was signed in 2018 and is valid through 2020. In April 2019, Flamengo and Fluminense came to an agreement with the state and the operators of the Maracanã to serve as joint-managers of the venue for the following six months, a deal which allowed the clubs to pay a fixed monthly fee and receive a higher share of matchday revenue than was granted under the previous deal.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

### Ilha do Urubu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Ilha do Urubu")]

Main article: [Estádio Luso Brasileiro](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Luso_Brasileiro "Estádio Luso Brasileiro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Ilha_do_urubu.jpeg/250px-Ilha_do_urubu.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Ilha_do_urubu.jpeg)

Ilha do Urubu

In 2017, Flamengo played their home matches at the **Estádio Luso Brasileiro** of [Portuguesa](/wiki/Associa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Atl%C3%A9tica_Portuguesa_(RJ) "Associação Atlética Portuguesa (RJ)") while disputing their stadium situation with the [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") state government and Complexo Maracanã Entretenimento S.A. (composed of [Odebrecht](/wiki/Odebrecht "Odebrecht"), IMX, [AEG](/wiki/Anschutz_Entertainment_Group "Anschutz Entertainment Group")), the operator of the Maracanã Stadium.[[136]](#cite_note-136) A three-year agreement was signed with Portuguesa over management of Estádio Luso Brasileiro, named **Ilha do Urubu** ("Vulture's Island") by Flamengo supporters in a poll.[[137]](#cite_note-137) The park was renovated to fit 20,500 spectators. Flamengo started playing at the arena in March 2017,[[138]](#cite_note-138) but after several delays and administrative issues and a new contract with the Maracanã, Flamengo broke their lease with the Ilha do Urubu in July 2018.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

### New Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: New Stadium")]

Main article: [New CR Flamengo Stadium](/wiki/New_CR_Flamengo_Stadium "New CR Flamengo Stadium")

In December 2024, it was reported that Flamengo released the design of their new stadium, an 80,000 capacity state-of-the-art stadium to be constructed at the Gasômetro area. The stadium is estimated to cost approximately €500 million and once completed, will become the largest stadium in Brazil. Scheduled for completion by November 2029 or earlier, the sports arena includes a North Stand dedicated for its most passionate supporters, luxury seating, a 360 degrees internal [LED](/wiki/Light-emitting_diode "Light-emitting diode") screen, a massive external display and a total of 6,200m2 of LED surfaces.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Rivalry with Botafogo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Rivalry with Botafogo")]

The match became known as the *Clássico da Rivaldade* ('Rivalry Classic') in the 1960s, is the traditional Brazilian derby between [Botafogo](/wiki/Botafogo_de_Futebol_e_Regatas "Botafogo de Futebol e Regatas") and Flamengo, both from [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). The first confrontation between Rio de Janeiro rivals Flamengo and Botafogo occurred in 1913. Flamengo's mascot of the vulture originated during the 1 June 1969, match against Botafogo when Flamengo supporters released a vulture onto the field in response to the racist cheers of *urubu* (vulture) from Botafogo and other teams' supporters.[[141]](#cite_note-141) Flamengo's top scorer in the derby is [Zico](/wiki/Zico_(footballer) "Zico (footballer)") and Botafogo's top scorer is [Heleno de Freitas](/wiki/Heleno_de_Freitas "Heleno de Freitas").

### Rivalry with Fluminense

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Rivalry with Fluminense")]

Main article: [Fla–Flu](/wiki/Fla%E2%80%93Flu "Fla–Flu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Campeonato_Carioca_-_Flamengo_-_Guerrero.jpg/250px-Campeonato_Carioca_-_Flamengo_-_Guerrero.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campeonato_Carioca_-_Flamengo_-_Guerrero.jpg)

[Paolo Guerrero](/wiki/Paolo_Guerrero "Paolo Guerrero") in a 2016 Campeonato Carioca game between Flamengo and [Fluminense](/wiki/Fluminense_FC "Fluminense FC")

The Fla–Flu is the traditional Brazilian derby between Flamengo and [Fluminense](/wiki/Fluminense_FC "Fluminense FC"), both from Rio de Janeiro. It is considered by football experts and much of the sports media as one of the greatest derbies in the world. The rivalry between these two clubs began in October 1911, when a group of dissatisfied players from Fluminense left their club and joined [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") club of Flamengo, establishing the football department at their new club. The first Fla–Flu ever was played the following year on 7 July. Fluminense won the match 3–2, with 800 people in attendance.[[142]](#cite_note-142) According to writer [Nelson Rodrigues](/wiki/Nelson_Rodrigues "Nelson Rodrigues"), the classic was generated by resentment. On the tricolor side, the fact that their starting players deserted and went to form Flamengo's football department, and on the red-black side, the fact that Fluminense still won the first match, circumstances that would have been fundamental in generating the derby mystique.[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144)

In 1950, the [Maracanã](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium") [Stadium](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium") was built to host the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup"), and it has been the stage for clashes between the two ever since. The stadium, which belongs to the Rio de Janeiro State Government, began to be managed by the two clubs in April 2019 for six months.[[145]](#cite_note-145) In September 2024, both clubs reached an agreement to jointly manage the stadium for another 20 years.[[146]](#cite_note-146) The Fla–Flu holds the world record for attendance in games between clubs: 194,603 spectators were present at the Maracanã Stadium, in the final of the [1963 Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/1963_Campeonato_Carioca "1963 Campeonato Carioca"), won by Flamengo after a goalless draw.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

Flamengo and Fluminense are the two most successful teams in the Campeonato Carioca: as of 2025, Flamengo have 39 state league titles and Fluminense have 34.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Since 2012, Fla–Flu has been considered an Intangible Heritage of Rio de Janeiro, being the only football derby to deserve this honor.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

### Rivalry with Vasco da Gama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Rivalry with Vasco da Gama")]

Main article: [Clássico dos Milhões](/wiki/Cl%C3%A1ssico_dos_Milh%C3%B5es "Clássico dos Milhões")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Flamengo_v_Vasco_September_2018_IMG_4563_%2842905616500%29.jpg/250px-Flamengo_v_Vasco_September_2018_IMG_4563_%2842905616500%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flamengo_v_Vasco_September_2018_IMG_4563_(42905616500).jpg)

Game between Flamengo and [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/CR_Vasco_da_Gama "CR Vasco da Gama") in 2018

The *Clássico dos Milhões* ('Derby of Millions') is the traditional Brazilian derby between Flamengo and [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/CR_Vasco_da_Gama "CR Vasco da Gama"), both from Rio de Janeiro. Although the rivalries against Botafogo and Fluminense are more traditional, Vasco is the club most hated by Flamengo supporters and *[vice versa](/wiki/Vice_versa "Vice versa")*,[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152) with the derby between them being considered one of the biggest rivalries in Brazilian football and in football worldwide. The derby's name originated in the 1920s and refers to the two largest fanbases in the state of Rio de Janeiro.[[153]](#cite_note-153) Both clubs were established in the late 19th century as regatta rowing clubs. The first football match between the clubs was played in 1923 when Vasco entered the top division of the [Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca "Campeonato Carioca").

From the 1972 to 2001, the matchup was elevated as the most important of Flamengo's rivalries and became one of the biggest rivalries in all of Brazil. In this span, Flamengo and Vasco played in or won the final of each of the phases of the state championship nearly every year, frequently facing one another. This also coincided with the beginnings of the national Campeonato Brasileiro and the growth in popularity of both clubs nationwide. The most iconic matches between Flamengo and Vasco featured the idols of both clubs challenging each other: [Zico](/wiki/Zico_(footballer) "Zico (footballer)") of Flamengo (1971–83; 85–89) and [Roberto Dinamite](/wiki/Roberto_Dinamite "Roberto Dinamite") of Vasco da Gama (1971–79; 80–93).

Some of the great players in Brazilian football played for both teams, and with hectic transfers in the 1980s and 1990s. [Bebeto](/wiki/Bebeto "Bebeto") revealed by Flamengo, was seen as Zico's successor, but left the red-black team and went to play for Vasco in 1989. [Romário](/wiki/Rom%C3%A1rio "Romário") revealed for Vasco's youth teams, when he returned to Brazil in 1995, recently champion of the [1994 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup") and elected [best player of the year by FIFA](/wiki/FIFA_World_Player_of_the_Year "FIFA World Player of the Year"), he chose to play for Flamengo. Other players like [Andrade](/wiki/Andrade_(footballer,_born_1957) "Andrade (footballer, born 1957)"), [Edmundo](/wiki/Edmundo_(footballer) "Edmundo (footballer)"), [Felipe](/wiki/Felipe_(footballer,_born_1977) "Felipe (footballer, born 1977)"), [Jean](/wiki/Jean_(footballer,_born_1982) "Jean (footballer, born 1982)"), [Jorginho](/wiki/Jorginho_(footballer,_born_1964) "Jorginho (footballer, born 1964)"), [Juninho Paulista](/wiki/Juninho_Paulista "Juninho Paulista"), [Dejan Petković](/wiki/Dejan_Petkovi%C4%87 "Dejan Petković") and [Tita](/wiki/Tita_(footballer,_born_1958) "Tita (footballer, born 1958)").

### Interstate Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Interstate Rivalries")]

#### Rivalry with Atlético Mineiro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Rivalry with Atlético Mineiro")]

Main article: [Flamengo–Atlético Mineiro football rivalry](/wiki/Flamengo%E2%80%93Atl%C3%A9tico_Mineiro_football_rivalry "Flamengo–Atlético Mineiro football rivalry")

Flamengo has an inter-state rivalry with [Atlético Mineiro](/wiki/Clube_Atl%C3%A9tico_Mineiro "Clube Atlético Mineiro") of [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"), developed in the 1980s from numerous controversial encounters between the two clubs in that decade's Campeonato Brasileiro and Copa Libertadores editions. It maintained its high intensity through the following years, and is considered one of the biggest interstate rivalries in Brazilian football.[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156)

#### Rivalry with Palmeiras

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Rivalry with Palmeiras")]

Main article: [Flamengo–Palmeiras football rivalry](/w/index.php?title=Flamengo%E2%80%93Palmeiras_football_rivalry&action=edit&redlink=1 "Flamengo–Palmeiras football rivalry (page does not exist)")

The rivalry with [Palmeiras](/wiki/SE_Palmeiras "SE Palmeiras") of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") has become increasingly intense over the course of the 21st century. The two clubs have already decided five official titles between them, including two Copa Libertadores, and they are the two most successful teams in South American football over the past decade. The rivalry extends beyond the pitch, with numerous clashes and exchanges of barbs between club officials. A considerable part of the Brazilian sports press claims that Flamengo and Palmeiras are the main protagonists of a “Spanish-style” dominance of Brazilian football, in which the two clubs share the major titles and polarize public attention.[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)

* record
* s shared record

(1) This championship was organized by the "Club of Thirteen," independently of the Brazilian Football Confederation (CBF), which organized a tournament for 16 of the clubs that were left out (including Guarani de Campinas, runner-up in 1986), called the Yellow Module, and which insisted on semifinals between the top two finishers of the Copa União (Flamengo and Internacional-RS) and those of the Yellow Module (Sport Recife and Guarani). Flamengo and Internacional did not participate, and the CBF registered Sport Recife and Guarani for the Copa Libertadores. From 2019 onwards, the CBF began to include the tournament among the Brazilian titles on its official website, but with different names: "Brasileiro" and "Copa União."[[161]](#cite_note-161)

(2) In 1940 the competition was interrupted with Flamengo and Fluminense in the lead, without the CBD making the title official, however, the clubs and newspapers at the time considered the result definitive and declared the Flamengo and Fluminense as the legitimate champions of the competition.[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163) The club currently considers itself champion of the competition and includes this title among its achievements.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

## Other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Other sports")]

### Men's basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Men's basketball")]

Main article: [Flamengo Basketball](/wiki/Flamengo_Basketball "Flamengo Basketball")

Flamengo basketball won the Rio de Janeiro City Championship in 1919 and have since grown to be one of the most successful and supported basketball teams in the country. The club have won six Brazilian Championships, a record 44 Rio de Janeiro State Championships, the 1953 South American Championship of Champions Clubs, and the 2009 South American League.[[167]](#cite_note-170)

In 2014, Flamengo won the League of the Americas without a single loss, defeating [Pinheiros](/wiki/Esporte_Clube_Pinheiros_(basketball) "Esporte Clube Pinheiros (basketball)") in the final.[[168]](#cite_note-171) This qualified Flamengo to their first Intercontinental Cup against [EuroLeague](/wiki/EuroLeague "EuroLeague") champions [Maccabi Tel Aviv](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv_B.C. "Maccabi Tel Aviv B.C."). Flamengo won and became the second Brazilian basketball team in history to be world champions.[[169]](#cite_note-172) Flamengo, Real Madrid and Barcelona are the only clubs to have won the Intercontinental Cups in both football and basketball.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

Flamengo hosted and participated in the [2019 FIBA Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/2019_FIBA_Intercontinental_Cup "2019 FIBA Intercontinental Cup"), falling to [BCL](/wiki/Basketball_Champions_League "Basketball Champions League") champions [AEK Athens](/wiki/AEK_B.C. "AEK B.C.") in the final.

### Women's football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Women's football")]

Main article: [Clube de Regatas do Flamengo (women)](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_(women) "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo (women)")

Between 1995 and 2001, the [Flamengo women's football team](/wiki/Clube_de_Regatas_do_Flamengo_(women) "Clube de Regatas do Flamengo (women)") competed in the Campeonato Carioca. In 2002 the women's Carioca tournament was not organized, and the club ceased operation of the team. Flamengo attempted to re-established their women's professional football department in 2011 through a partnership with the city of [Guarujá](/wiki/Guaruj%C3%A1 "Guarujá") where the team trained and hoped to sign [Marta](/wiki/Marta_(footballer) "Marta (footballer)"), but the team never materialized.[[171]](#cite_note-174)[[172]](#cite_note-175) In 2015 president Eduardo Bandeira de Mello succeeded in establishing the football team through a partnership with the [Brazilian Navy](/wiki/Brazilian_Navy "Brazilian Navy"). In their first season, the team won the [women's Campeonato Carioca](/wiki/Campeonato_Carioca_de_Futebol_Feminino "Campeonato Carioca de Futebol Feminino") state championship and have won it every season from 2015 to 2019. In 2016 Flamengo won the [Campeonato Brasileiro de Futebol Feminino](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_de_Futebol_Feminino "Campeonato Brasileiro de Futebol Feminino") for the first time against [Rio Preto](/wiki/Rio_Preto_Esporte_Clube "Rio Preto Esporte Clube"), become the only club outside the state of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)") to win the tournament since its creation in 2013.[[173]](#cite_note-176) Flamengo also competed in the 2016 and 2017 [Copa do Brasil de Futebol Feminino](/wiki/Copa_do_Brasil_de_Futebol_Feminino "Copa do Brasil de Futebol Feminino") before the cancellation of the competition in favor of the Campeonato Brasileiro.

### Women's basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Women's basketball")]

The Flamengo [women's basketball](/wiki/Women%27s_basketball "Women's basketball") team won back-to-back Brazilian championships in 1954 and 1955. Ten years later with some of the same players, the program won back-to-back Brazilian titles again in 1964 and 1965. Flamengo players Norminha, Angelina, Marlene and Delei were champions of the [1967 Pan American Games](/wiki/1967_Pan_American_Games "1967 Pan American Games") in Winnipeg with the [Brazil women's national basketball team](/wiki/Brazil_women%27s_national_basketball_team "Brazil women's national basketball team").

In 1966 Flamengo won the Inter-club Basketball World Championship. The team was led by Angelina, considered one of the best players of her time.[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### Rowing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Rowing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/1896_-_Flamengo.jpg/250px-1896_-_Flamengo.jpg)](/wiki/File:1896_-_Flamengo.jpg)

One of the firsts [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") teams of the club, in 1896.

The "Flamengo Regatta Group", later renamed the "Flamengo Regatta Club", was established in 1895 as Flamengo's first ever organized athletic department, forming the basis of the club's history and identity to this day. The first [regatta](/wiki/Regatta "Regatta") victory came in 1898 in the Nautical Championship of Brazil, and the first title was won in 1900, the Regatta of the IV Centenary of the Discovery of Brazil, for which the club was awarded the Jug Tropon trophy. In 1905 the club won a classic event, the South American Cup. By 1908, Flamengo had already won 43 gold, 126 silver and 141 bronze medals. The success of the rowing club made the team famous even before the founding of the football department in 1911. Great rowers such as Everardo Paes de Lima, Arnaldo Voigt, Alfredo Correia ("Boca Larga"), Ângelo Gammaro ("Angelú") and Antônio Rebello Junior ("Engole Garfo") came through Flamengo, the latter three being considered Brazilian sports heroes for completing the Rio-Santos crossing in 1932.

From 1931 to 1937 Flamengo were seven-time champions of Rio de Janeiro, and were four-time repeat state champions from 1940 to 1943. In 1963 the "Buck era" began, which revolutionized Flamengo rowing. The coach brought in athletes from other states and renovated the club's facilities to better accommodate the boats. Buck coached the Brazil national team, directing the team in several international competitions. In the early 1980s, Flamengo won the state championship and won again in 1992. The club has won the men's Brazil Trophy 10 times, and the female once, in addition to 45 Carioca state titles.

### Water polo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Water polo")]

[Water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo") is the second oldest sport practiced by the club, after rowing. The team played their first game on 27 May 1913, in Rio de Janeiro, and defeated Clube Internacional de Regatas, 3–2. Flamengo only opened its water sports facility in 1965. Prior to that, athletes played and trained in the [Rodrigo de Freitas Lagoon](/wiki/Rodrigo_de_Freitas_Lagoon "Rodrigo de Freitas Lagoon") or in the sea. Flamengo's first polo championship in Rio de Janeiro came in 1985 and was the start of a run of nine consecutive championships through 1993. In 1985 the club won the South American Club Championship and the Brazil Trophy (also won four consecutive times). A female water polo team was established in 1987, winning the Brazil Trophy in 1987 and 1991 and the state championship in 1995.

### American football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: American football")]

The club launched their [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team in 2013, forming a partnership with the Rio de Janeiro Emperors. The Emperors were established in 2008 and had previously partnered with [Fluminense](/wiki/Fluminense_FC "Fluminense FC") from 2010 to 2013. The team officially goes by the name of the Flamengo Emperors and compete in the BFA (*Brasil Futebol Americano*).[[175]](#cite_note-178)

### Tennis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Tennis")]

Flamengo began playing tennis championships in 1916 and became three-time Rio champions soon after (1916–18), even with their athletes training at other clubs. Until 1932 the club practiced tennis on their football field at the Rua Paysandu. In 1963 the club inaugurated their own facilities and courts. The biggest idol of Flamengo's tennis department is [Thomas Koch](/wiki/Thomaz_Koch "Thomaz Koch").

### E-sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CR_Flamengo&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: E-sports")]

Main article: [Flamengo eSports](/wiki/Flamengo_eSports "Flamengo eSports")

In 2017 the club announced they would be entering the increasingly popular [e-sports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") leagues the following year, beginning with a [League of Legends](/wiki/League_of_Legends "League of Legends") department and eventually establishing a [PES](/wiki/Pro_Evolution_Soccer "Pro Evolution Soccer") team. Because the competitive League of Legends center of Brazil is at the [Riot Games](/wiki/Riot_Games "Riot Games") studio in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), Flamengo established a permanent "gaming office" for the team in the city.[[176]](#cite_note-179) Flamengo announced that they would not be partnering with an existing team but rather would have their own team. In October 2017 they announced the purchase of Merciless Gaming, a team in the second division of the Brazilian League of Legends championship.[[177]](#cite_note-180)
